findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an in-session proving command (e.g., a unit test asserting no ticket-event-writer call is made when `derive_plan_material_pin_health` returns advisory state) and split the 'child cb6b surfaces' clause into a separate deferred AC.
evidence: The Verification section lists: test_attestation_invalidation.py, test_plan_review_resign.py, test_plan_review_sidecar.py, test_drift_refresh_candidate.py, test_plan_material_pin_config.py, test_compute_validity_callers.py — none covers ticket event store absence.
No implementation step describes guarding against event-store writes for derived health, so there is no code path for a test to exercise.
The second half of the AC ('child cb6b surfaces') defers surfacing responsibility to a sibling, making the AC a hybrid in-session / deferred criterion without separation.
findings.finding: The AC 'No warning event is persisted in the ticket event store for derived health; advisory state is exposed through the returned health contract and child cb6b surfaces' has no associated test file, pytest command, or proving mechanism in the Verification section. It is a negative runtime assertion about the ticket event store with no in-session check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'No warning event is persisted in the ticket event store for derived health'
scenarios: An executor writes derive_plan_material_pin_health in a way that incidentally emits a ticket event on advisory drift; nothing in the test suite catches it.
The 'child cb6b surfaces' clause implies this AC is only partially completable in-session, but the AC presents it as a single completable item.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into two ACs: (1) 'derive_plan_material_pin_health never calls the ticket event writer; verified by asserting no call to <event_writer_function> in test_attestation_invalidation.py or test_plan_material_pin_config.py'; (2) '[deferred to child cb6b] Advisory health state is surfaced in audit/UI rendering.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the function name exported from this story with the name child 2bbd imports: either update the plan to export a `compute_plan_review_validity` alias/wrapper (or rename) that child 2bbd can call, or explicitly note that child 2bbd's reference to `compute_plan_review_validity` should be updated to `compute_validity` with `profile=PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE`.
evidence: Plan: 'Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None, profile=PlanValidityProfile.DEFAULT) without renaming or wrapping it'
Child 2bbd ticket description: 'call compute_plan_review_validity(profile=close) only when verify.require_plan_review_for_close is true'
No function named `compute_plan_review_validity` exists in the codebase (search confirmed 0 matches)
Child 2bbd has `depends_on` this story (75de-02b0-d454-410b) — it is a direct downstream consumer
findings.finding: The plan defines `compute_validity` as the single extended function (with `profile=PlanValidityProfile.DEFAULT`), but child 2bbd (a direct dependent of this story) references `compute_plan_review_validity(profile=close)` — a different function name. This interface mismatch between what this plan produces and what child 2bbd consumes means a conforming implementation of both stories will have a broken call site in `transition_close`.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 5: 'Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None, profile=PlanValidityProfile.DEFAULT)'; child 2bbd description: 'call compute_plan_review_validity(profile=close)'
scenarios: An implementer follows this plan and extends `compute_validity`; child 2bbd's implementer calls `compute_plan_review_validity` which does not exist, causing a NameError at close time
Or: an implementer follows 2bbd and creates a `compute_plan_review_validity` wrapper, but this plan explicitly forbids renaming or wrapping `compute_validity` — creating a spec conflict
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line alias `compute_plan_review_validity = compute_validity` to `attest.py`'s public exports, or update child 2bbd's spec to call `compute_validity` directly with `profile=PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE`. The alias approach is lower blast-radius and can be removed once 2bbd is implemented.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the leaf's function-name decision with the parent's specification: either rename `compute_validity` to `compute_plan_review_validity` as the parent specifies, OR update the parent first to ratify keeping `compute_validity`, then re-review the leaf against the corrected parent.
evidence: Parent (jira-reb-1160) Enforcement section: 'compute_plan_review_validity preserves its top-level {valid, reason, verdict} contract and adds health: DerivedPlanReviewHealth'

Leaf Implementation fifth bullet: 'Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None, profile=PlanValidityProfile.DEFAULT) without renaming or wrapping it'
Parent names the unified boundary as compute_plan_review_validity consistently; the leaf redefines the deliverable by keeping the old name
findings.finding: The leaf keeps the validity function named `compute_validity` and explicitly says 'without renaming or wrapping it', but the parent epic names the unified boundary `compute_plan_review_validity` ('compute_plan_review_validity preserves its top-level {valid, reason, verdict} contract and adds health: DerivedPlanReviewHealth'). The leaf contradicts the parent's specified symbol name for the shared contract.
findings.location: Implementation section, fifth bullet: 'Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(...) without renaming or wrapping it'
scenarios: Sibling stories (close: 2bbd, surfaces: cb6b) coded against the parent's named boundary compute_plan_review_validity will not find that symbol when they integrate with this story's output.
A reviewer of cb6b or 2bbd checking against the parent contract will see a discrepancy with what this story delivers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` definition with the parent's complete contract: either include a `phase_status` stub field (e.g. typed but defaulting to `None` until 5967 populates it) so the struct matches the parent's authoritative shape, OR update the parent to formally split the contract definition between this story and 5967 before re-reviewing the leaf.
evidence: Parent (jira-reb-1160) Enforcement section: 'That nested contract has pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned; phase_status exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed; enforced boolean; and targets: list[TargetPinDetail]'
Leaf omits phase_status from the DerivedPlanReviewHealth definition and defers to child 5967
Parent AC: 'All detailed review surfaces consume the shared derived-health contract' — implies the contract is complete when consumers integrate
findings.finding: The leaf's `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` contract omits `phase_status` (deferring it to child 5967), but the parent epic specifies the complete nested contract atomically: 'That nested contract has pin_status ... phase_status exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed; enforced boolean; and targets: list[TargetPinDetail].' The leaf ships a structurally incomplete contract relative to the parent's authoritative specification.
findings.location: Implementation section, second bullet: 'It has pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned; enforced: bool; and targets: list[TargetPinDetail]'
scenarios: Child cb6b (surfaces) is expected to consume the shared derived-health contract per the parent's AC; if this story ships the contract without phase_status, cb6b either cannot implement phase health rendering or must wait for 5967 to amend the contract shape this story already owns.
Integration tests written against the parent's full contract shape will fail against the partial contract this story delivers.

evidence: 27 acceptance-criteria items (> 25)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a co-located per-profile suppression table to the `PlanValidityProfile` definition listing exactly which `compute_validity` check branches each profile suppresses (e.g. `CLOSE`: suppress dep-hash AND whole-HEAD freshness; `DRIFT_REFRESH`: suppress dep-hash AND whole-HEAD freshness; `DEFAULT`: suppress none).
evidence: Plan text: '`CLOSE` and `DRIFT_REFRESH` deliberately share one rule in this story—disable only code freshness—but remain distinct call-site intents'

Child 2bbd (ticket 2bbd-87f6-8765-4b00) describes the close profile as 'deliberately exclude per-file dependency hashes and whole-HEAD/code freshness' — a slightly different framing than this plan's 'disable only code freshness'.
No co-located table or structured `profile → suppressed_checks` mapping appears in either the `compute_validity` signature spec or a dedicated enum-adjacent decision rule block.
An implementer must reconcile the prose of this plan with child 2bbd's description to know what 'code freshness' means: both dep-hash and whole-HEAD checks, or only the per-path dep-hash branch.
findings.finding: The `PlanValidityProfile` suppression rule ('disable only code freshness' for `CLOSE` and `DRIFT_REFRESH`) is stated in prose but never co-located with the `compute_validity` signature extension as a per-profile check-suppression table, leaving the implementation contract fragmented between this plan and child 2bbd.
findings.location: Implementation section, bullet 5: `PlanValidityProfile` definition and `compute_validity` extension
scenarios: Implementer disables dep-hash check for CLOSE but not the whole-HEAD fallback branch, creating an asymmetry — the plan phrase 'disable only code freshness' covers both but is not explicit.
Child 2bbd adds additional CLOSE-only checks (phase compatibility) that conflict with the 'share one rule' assertion here.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the sidecar extension bullet whether `build_payload` bumps the schema to `plan_review_result_v3` and whether `latest_review_result`'s schema guard must be updated to accept the new version, with a corresponding AC line.
evidence: Plan text: 'Extend the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar payload with the exact review-time `PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material` records... Serialize `reviewed_related_material` as an ordered list'

`sidecar.py::latest_review_result` at line 149-152 hard-codes: `payload.get('schema') in ('plan_review_result_v1', 'plan_review_result_v2')` — if the schema string is bumped to v3, existing readers will silently skip new sidecars until updated.
`sidecar.py::build_payload` at line 480: `'schema': 'plan_review_result_v2'` — no guidance in the plan on whether to bump this.
The AC 'Review sidecars persist the exact pre-LLM `related_material` snapshot; pre-feature sidecars behave as legacy-unpinned' does not specify schema version behavior.
findings.finding: The plan extends `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar payload with `reviewed_related_material` but never specifies whether `sidecar.build_payload` bumps the schema version string (currently `plan_review_result_v2`), nor whether `latest_review_result`'s schema guard needs updating — leaving the reader/writer version contract ambiguous.
findings.location: Implementation section, bullet 8: `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar payload extension
scenarios: Author bumps to v3 but forgets to update `latest_review_result`'s guard — resign.py reads None from the sidecar, falls into 'no sidecar found' path, and returns an unhelpful 'run rebar review-plan' error for every re-sign attempt.

Author does not bump the schema — the new field coexists with v2 schema, which is fine functionally, but `all_review_results` in metrics and calibration code may deserialize the new field unexpectedly.
findings.suggested_fix: Since `reviewed_related_material` is additive and backward-compatible, recommend keeping `plan_review_result_v2` and adding a note: 'The field is additive to v2; the schema string remains `plan_review_result_v2`. `latest_review_result` accepts v2 sidecars with or without the field; an absent field yields legacy-unpinned per the existing absent-key rule.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the dependency on child acaf explicitly (e.g., 'this story's execution requires child acaf to be merged first') and describe what the test does when child acaf's code is absent (skip, xfail, or block).
evidence: 'sign_plan_review still emits the complete current pin set as owned by child acaf' — Implementation section explicitly attributes pin emission to child acaf.

'using the already-declared dependency on child acaf's emission path' — the plan references an external dependency but neither names the dependency edge formally nor explains what 'already-declared' refers to in this plan document.
If child acaf's emission path is not yet merged, the AC cannot be evaluated in-session.
findings.finding: The AC 'tests/unit/test_plan_material_pin_config.py proves sign_plan_review emits identical complete pin lines with enforcement true and false, using the already-declared dependency on child acaf's emission path' exercises a code path owned by sibling ticket child acaf, with no explicit dependency edge declared in this plan (VCDT defect #9 — references a sibling ticket's files with no dependency edge).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'test_plan_material_pin_config.py proves sign_plan_review emits identical complete pin lines'
scenarios: child acaf's pin-emission code is not present when this story executes; test_plan_material_pin_config.py fails or is vacuous because it has nothing to exercise.
The 'already-declared dependency' is documented elsewhere (e.g., a parent epic), invisible to an executor reading only this plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Dependencies' section or note: 'Depends on child acaf (pin emission in sign_plan_review) being merged. test_plan_material_pin_config.py should be marked xfail or skipped when running without child acaf's changes; the AC is only provable after child acaf merges.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly confirm (or enforce in tests) that the sidecar reader tolerates unknown fields so rolled-back code silently ignores `reviewed_related_material`; if the reader is strict, add an expand-contract step (deploy lenient reader first, then write new field).
evidence: 'Rollback removes the additive health key and its consumers; no stored-data migration is required.'
Plan does not state that sidecar deserialization is lenient (e.g., extra-fields-ignored) on the read path, nor does it point to existing evidence that the reader is tolerant of unknown fields.
Absence: no expand-contract sequencing (write new field only after confirming reader is tolerant) is described for the sidecar format change.
findings.finding: The plan extends the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar with `reviewed_related_material` and states 'no stored-data migration is required' on rollback, but does not explicitly confirm that rolled-back (pre-feature) code will safely tolerate new sidecars containing the field. If the sidecar reader uses strict/exact-key schema validation, rolled-back code encountering a new sidecar written by the feature branch could raise a parse error rather than ignoring the unknown field.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload' section and Rollback statement in the health key paragraph
scenarios: A developer enables the feature, signs a new sidecar with `reviewed_related_material`, then rolls back the codebase. The rolled-back sidecar reader encounters the new field and raises a schema validation error, breaking re-sign recovery for all sidecars written during the feature window.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC item: '`tests/unit/test_plan_review_sidecar.py` proves that a sidecar containing an unrecognized top-level key is parsed without error by the current reader, confirming rollback safety.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation task (ADR entry, AGENTS.md section, or design note) capturing the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` extension protocol, `PlanValidityProfile` call-site-intent invariant, and the fail-open per-key rollout pattern before child stories begin implementation.
evidence: 'Child 5967 extends the same contract with phase_status and phase compatibility after it introduces the phase manifest format.'
'child 2bbd later wires CLOSE into transition_close, while child cb6b owns audit and UI rendering calls'
'CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH deliberately share one rule in this story...but remain distinct call-site intents so later close policy cannot silently change progressive refresh.'
Absence: no mention of ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design doc recording these decisions for downstream child-story implementors.
findings.finding: The plan introduces three novel architectural decisions — (1) `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` as a multi-consumer contract explicitly designed for extension by child stories 5967, 2bbd, and cb6b; (2) `PlanValidityProfile` as a new call-site-scoped policy enum with explicit future extension points; (3) a fail-open per-key rollout pattern modeled on `mcp_readonly()` — but does not mention updating any ADR, AGENTS.md, or design doc. Child story authors need a canonical reference for how to extend the contract.
findings.location: Implementation — `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` contract paragraph; `PlanValidityProfile` paragraph; `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins` paragraph
scenarios: Child story 5967 author must infer the extension protocol for `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` from the parent story prose alone; risk of diverging contract implementations across child stories if no canonical doc exists.
A future engineer adds a new `PlanValidityProfile` value without understanding the 'distinct call-site intents' invariant, silently coupling close and refresh policy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that sidecar I/O exceptions in `resign_plan_review` (file unreadable, truncated, unparseable JSON) are caught, treated as `malformed-pin` (or a named error state), and refuse re-sign with a structured log event rather than propagating an unhandled exception.
evidence: 'resign_plan_review does not call compute_validity ... It retains its existing sidecar verdict and own-material-fingerprint guards, reads reviewed_related_material, and invokes derive_plan_material_pin_health'
Plan specifies 'any present invalid value produces malformed-pin' and 'an absent field alone is legacy-unpinned' but is silent on the case where the sidecar read itself throws an I/O exception
findings.finding: The `resign_plan_review` path reads `reviewed_related_material` from the sidecar but the plan only specifies handling for malformed field values and absent fields — it does not specify what happens when the sidecar file itself cannot be read (I/O exception thrown before any field parsing). The existing own-material guard presumably has its own I/O error handling, but the new `reviewed_related_material` read is described as an independent step with no explicit exception handler.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Edit `resign.py`' paragraph; AC: 'Re-sign preserves the existing own-material staleness guard and never passes a sidecar payload to `compute_validity`'
scenarios: Sidecar file is on a network mount that becomes unavailable after the guard check but before the `reviewed_related_material` read — unhandled exception propagates to caller
Partial sidecar write (crash during sign) leaves a truncated file that causes a JSON decode error distinct from a field-level validation failure
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the `resign.py` implementation paragraph: 'Any I/O or JSON-parse exception raised while reading the sidecar is treated as `malformed-pin`; re-sign is refused and a structured warning event `plan_material_pin_sidecar_unreadable` is emitted rather than propagating the exception.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Motivation / Current Behavior' section that concretely states what goes wrong today without pin enforcement (e.g., a scenario where drift goes undetected, a named gap in the existing validity pipeline) or a failing test/repro that the implementation fixes.
evidence: Goal: 'Use signed pins as the single related-material validity check across every attestation lifecycle consumer' — describes the desired end state, not a problem with the current state.
ABSENCE: no repro steps, no failing test, no named defect, no user/system problem statement anywhere in the plan text.
findings.finding: The plan adds a significant pin-enforcement mechanism (new config key, helper, health contract, sidecar field, re-sign guard) without demonstrating that any current behavior is wrong or insufficient — there is no reproduction, no Expected-vs-Actual, and no named defect or user-observable gap the current code causes.
findings.location: Goal section / Implementation (entire plan)
scenarios: A reviewer or implementer cannot assess whether the complexity cost of the mechanism is justified without knowing what observable failure or gap it addresses.
If the mechanism is already partially present in the codebase, the plan cannot detect the redundancy without a 'current behavior is X' baseline.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Current Behavior' paragraph: e.g., 'Today, compute_validity performs no comparison between the fingerprints recorded in signed manifest pins and the live material fingerprints; a PR that silently alters a prerequisite passes all claim checks. This plan adds enforcement so such drift invalidates the attestation when enabled.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE` ships with at least one wired call site in this story, OR accept it as a deliberate 0-call-site seed and document that explicitly in a code comment (rather than relying on the plan prose) so it is not flagged as dead code by linters or future reviewers.
evidence: Plan: 'child 2bbd later wires CLOSE into transition_close' — CLOSE has zero call sites at ship time

Plan: 'CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH deliberately share one rule in this story—disable only code freshness—but remain distinct call-site intents so later close policy cannot silently change progressive refresh'
The justification for keeping CLOSE distinct is explicitly forward-looking ('so later close policy cannot silently change'), not a current done-definition need
Rule-of-Three: at ship time CLOSE has 0 call sites; DRIFT_REFRESH has exactly 1 (drift_refresh_candidate); only DEFAULT has ≥3
findings.finding: PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE ships as dead code at this story's boundary — it has zero wired call sites (its only named consumer, transition_close, is explicitly deferred to child 2bbd). At ship time CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH are specified with identical behavior ('disables only code freshness'), making the three-value enum effectively a two-value enum (DEFAULT vs. not-DEFAULT) with an unused third member.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Define PlanValidityProfile with exactly DEFAULT, CLOSE, and DRIFT_REFRESH … child 2bbd later wires CLOSE into transition_close'
scenarios: An implementer must maintain a dead enum value and ensure it doesn't accidentally collide with DRIFT_REFRESH behavior until child 2bbd arrives.
If child 2bbd is delayed or descoped, CLOSE stays dead indefinitely.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) defer defining `PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE` to child 2bbd (which owns the only call site), keeping this story's enum as `DEFAULT | DRIFT_REFRESH`; or (b) add an inline code comment in `attest.py` explaining that CLOSE is intentionally pre-defined with no current callers to prevent child 2bbd from having to change the enum's import surface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prerequisite ordering statement (or a concrete fallback/stub interface) for `PlanMaterialPin`, `PlanRelationSnapshot`, and `reviewed_related_material` from child `acaf` — the plan currently treats these as available but the codebase confirms they do not yet exist.
evidence: Grep for PlanMaterialPin, PlanRelationSnapshot, reviewed_related_material in src/rebar: all return no matches
Grep for derive_plan_material_pin_health in src/rebar: no matches
Ticket acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c signature shows 'dep absent src/rebar/llm/plan_review/relation_snapshot.py', confirming the module does not yet exist
Plan says 'child acaf's emission path' as if already delivered, but acaf status is 'open'
findings.finding: The plan asserts `PlanMaterialPin`, `PlanRelationSnapshot`, `reviewed_related_material`, and `derive_plan_material_pin_health` are available inputs — but all are deliverables of sibling child `acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c`, which is currently `open` with `relation_snapshot.py` marked `dep absent` in its attestation. The plan builds on these symbols without stating a hard prerequisite ordering or a fallback if acaf ships late.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add one shared derive_plan_material_pin_health(...) helper in attest.py. It accepts parsed signed-manifest pins or equivalent review-time sidecar records' and 'Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload with the exact review-time PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material records'
scenarios: If this story is implemented before acaf delivers relation_snapshot.py, the `PlanMaterialPin` type and `PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material` it depends on don't exist, making the sidecar payload extension and the helper function unimplementable.

AC: 'tests/unit/test_plan_material_pin_config.py proves sign_plan_review emits identical complete pin lines with enforcement true and false, using the already-declared dependency on child acaf's emission path' cannot be satisfied if acaf hasn't shipped.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit action item to regenerate `tests/golden/config_surface.json` after adding `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` to `_SECTIONS["verify"]` (the existing golden-update test only catches removals, not additions).
evidence: tests/golden/config_surface.json confirmed to exist and does NOT contain 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins'
test_config_surface_golden.py:47-59 confirms the test only fails when a pinned key is REMOVED without a tombstone — it does NOT fail when a new key is added but not in the golden

test_config_surface_golden.py:9-10: 'It intentionally does NOT fail when a NEW live key is absent from the golden (adding a surface is not a regression); regenerate the golden deliberately when you add one'
findings.finding: The plan states 'the config-surface golden records the key' when describing `enforce_plan_material_pins` registration, but the golden file `tests/golden/config_surface.json` is a static pinned artifact that must be explicitly updated — adding `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` to `_SECTIONS["verify"]` alone will not cause `test_config_surface_golden.py` to fail (it only catches *removals*, not additions). The plan implies the golden update is automatic but it requires a deliberate manual regeneration step.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Register enforce_plan_material_pins: bool = False on _config_schema.py::VerifyConfig ... the config-surface golden records the key'
scenarios: A developer registers the key in _SECTIONS but forgets to regenerate tests/golden/config_surface.json; all tests pass silently, but the golden no longer accurately reflects the live surface.
The plan's statement 'the config-surface golden records the key' reads as automatic but is actually a manual step that must be explicitly called out.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section: 'Regenerate `tests/golden/config_surface.json` by running `python -m rebar._commands.show_config --regenerate-golden` (or equivalent) so `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` and its canonical env var appear in the golden.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/golden/config_surface.json` as an explicit edit target and include a step to regenerate it after adding `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` to `_SECTIONS['verify']`.
evidence: tests/golden/config_surface.json is a static file listing every config key; adding verify.enforce_plan_material_pins to _SECTIONS without updating the golden causes test_config_surface_golden.py to fail
The golden file is not named anywhere in the Implementation or Verification sections as an edit target
findings.finding: The plan says 'the config-surface golden records the key' after adding `enforce_plan_material_pins` to `_SECTIONS['verify']`, but `tests/golden/config_surface.json` is a golden file that must be explicitly regenerated/updated as a deliverable — it is not updated automatically. The plan does not name `tests/golden/config_surface.json` as an edit target.
findings.location: Implementation section — config registration bullet; AC referencing the config-surface golden
scenarios: Developer adds enforce_plan_material_pins to _SECTIONS but forgets to regenerate tests/golden/config_surface.json; test_config_surface_golden.py fails CI.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation: 'Regenerate `tests/golden/config_surface.json` by running the golden-update command (see its `_comment` field) after adding the key to `_SECTIONS["verify"]`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` as an edit target for threading `reviewed_related_material` from the review flow into `sidecar.emit` / `build_payload`.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:608: sidecar.emit(verdict, material=material, repo_root=repo_root) — the sole production caller
Plan names sidecar.py as an edit target (Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload) but does not name __init__.py
The related_material snapshot is collected by child acaf before the LLM; it must be threaded from the review flow through to sidecar.emit
findings.finding: The plan extends `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar payloads with `reviewed_related_material` by modifying `sidecar.build_payload`, but the only production caller of `sidecar.emit` (which calls `build_payload`) is `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:608`. The plan does not enumerate `__init__.py` as an edit target for passing the new field through. If `build_payload`'s signature gains a new required `reviewed_related_material` parameter, all callers must be updated.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload' bullet
scenarios: build_payload gains a reviewed_related_material parameter; __init__.py:608's call to sidecar.emit does not pass it; either the field is silently absent (defeating the feature) or a TypeError is raised at runtime.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py` to the plan's new-file creation list (alongside the other new test files) and add at least one AC that names it as the proving vehicle for the resign sidecar-pin scenarios.
evidence: Directory listing of tests/unit shows no test_plan_review_resign.py
Verification section: 'pytest -q tests/unit/test_attestation_invalidation.py tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py ...'

The plan does not include test_plan_review_resign.py in its AC-named new test files (only test_plan_material_pin_config.py, test_drift_refresh_candidate.py, test_compute_validity_callers.py are mentioned in ACs)
findings.finding: The plan's Verification section names `tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py` (a new file) as a proving target, but there is currently no such file in `tests/unit/`. The Verification `pytest` command lists it, so the test run will fail unless this file is created. The plan does not include it in its AC list of named new test files.
findings.location: Verification section — pytest command listing test_plan_review_resign.py
scenarios: Implementer runs the exact pytest command from Verification; test_plan_review_resign.py is missing; pytest errors with 'file not found' rather than a test failure.

findings.finding: `drift_refresh_candidate`'s refactor to delegate to `compute_validity(kind=plan-review, profile=DRIFT_REFRESH)` has a mechanistic gap: `compute_validity` requires a `ticket_state` dict (used for reopen invalidation check at line 511 and for material fingerprint at line 604), but `drift_refresh_candidate` currently performs its own `signing.verify_signature()` call and constructs no ticket_state. The plan says to call `compute_validity` on 'the verified attestation result' but never specifies how ticket_state is obtained in the new code. Without it, the reopen check inside `compute_validity` receives an empty dict and silently skips the reopen guard — breaking the 'reopened attestations are not progressive-refresh candidates' requirement stated in the ACs.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'In `drift_refresh_candidate`, call `compute_validity(kind=plan-review, profile=DRIFT_REFRESH)` on the verified attestation result'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_reads.show_ticket(ticket_id, repo_root=repo_root)` call inside `drift_refresh_candidate` (with an exception handler returning `None`) to supply `ticket_state` to `compute_validity`; note this in the implementation section alongside the guard-removal description.

findings.finding: The two ACs 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true leaves new pin emission enabled' and 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=false leaves new pin emission enabled' are listed in this story but pin emission is explicitly owned by child acaf ('Normal sign_plan_review still emits the complete current pin set as owned by child acaf'). No step in this plan creates or modifies the emission path, and no test file in the verification section is scoped to verify emission is unaffected. The executor has no in-session proving command to satisfy these ACs within this story's deliverables.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true leaves new pin emission enabled' and 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=false leaves new pin emission enabled'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a dependency edge 'this story depends on child acaf for sign_plan_review emission' and relocate both ACs to child acaf; OR add a targeted test in `test_plan_material_pin_config.py` that stubs `sign_plan_review`, calls it with the flag in both states, and asserts the returned/recorded pin set is identical in both cases.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name a specific test file (e.g., `tests/unit/test_derive_plan_material_pin_health.py`) in the Verification section and map each failure-path AC (stale-pin-missing variants, all three failure_kind values, severity ordering, archived-target comparison, duplicate role+ID) to concrete test cases in that file.
evidence: AC: 'Missing, deleted, unreadable, or reducer-failing targets produce stale-pin-missing; non-missing read failures emit plan_material_pin_target_unreadable without raising' — named behavior with no attributed test file.
AC: 'Archived readable targets compare normally' — named behavior with no attributed test file.
AC: 'Duplicate role-plus-ID pins produce malformed-pin' — named behavior with no attributed test file.
AC: 'Malformed manifest or sidecar pin metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising' — named behavior with no attributed test file.
ABSENCE: no test file (e.g., test_derive_plan_material_pin_health.py) is named in the verification section for the helper's unit-level contract.
findings.finding: The plan's core new helper `derive_plan_material_pin_health` has rich failure-path branching (five pin_status values, three failure_kind variants for structured warnings, severity aggregation ordering, archived vs. deleted target distinction, duplicate role+ID detection) but no test file is named or specified to cover these unit-level paths; the pytest command lists `test_plan_review_sidecar.py` without stating what it covers.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Add one shared derive_plan_material_pin_health helper'; Acceptance Criteria lines for stale-pin-missing, archived targets, duplicate role+ID, malformed pin; Verification section pytest command
scenarios: The helper's severity aggregation ordering (malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current) could be inverted or misimplemented; without a dedicated unit test the error surfaces only through integration-level tests that may not enumerate all orderings.
The three failure_kind values (resolution, io, reducer) for plan_material_pin_target_unreadable could be silently collapsed; no test file is specified to verify each variant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the failure_kind mapping explicitly: e.g., 'resolution = target-ID lookup or registry resolution failure; io = filesystem/read error; reducer = fingerprint computation or hash-reduction failure.'
evidence: Plan says 'failure_kind exactly resolution, io, or reducer' with no further definition of which exceptions or conditions map to each kind.

An executor must decide whether, e.g., a FileNotFoundError from the reducer is 'io' or 'reducer', or whether a missing registry entry is 'resolution' or 'missing' (already handled as stale-pin-missing).
findings.finding: The `failure_kind` taxonomy (`resolution`, `io`, `reducer`) for the structured warning event `plan_material_pin_target_unreadable` is named but the mapping from specific exception/code-path types to each kind is not stated, leaving boundary decisions to executor judgment.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Every non-absence failure emits structured warning event plan_material_pin_target_unreadable with failure_kind exactly resolution, io, or reducer'
scenarios: Two implementors produce different mappings, making test fixtures non-interchangeable across the test suite.
A reducer exception is logged as 'io' by mistake; the AC that checks failure_kind passes because it only checks that the event fires, not the kind value.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Implementation bullet: 'resolution covers target-ID lookup and registry failures; io covers filesystem and read errors; reducer covers fingerprint computation and hash-reduction failures.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins(repo_root)` should call `load_config(repo_root)` (passing `repo_root` through) or whether it should drop the `repo_root` parameter entirely to match the `mcp_readonly()` model — the plan currently implies both patterns simultaneously.
evidence: load_config() signature confirmed at config.py:354: `def load_config(root: str | os.PathLike[str] | None = None, ...)`
mcp_readonly() confirmed at config.py:402 calls `load_config()` with no root — it has no repo_root parameter either
Plan says the reader is 'modeled on mcp_readonly()' but also takes a `repo_root` parameter, which mcp_readonly() does not
findings.finding: The plan says `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins(repo_root) -> bool` reads `load_config().verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` (modeled on `mcp_readonly()`), but `load_config()` accepts `root` as its first positional argument. The `mcp_readonly()` model does not take `repo_root` and therefore correctly omits it. If the new function accepts `repo_root` but then calls `load_config()` without passing it, the function signature is misleading — the `repo_root` parameter is silently ignored for config discovery, causing the function to always read the CWD-resolved config regardless of the caller-supplied root.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add _read_enforce_plan_material_pins(repo_root) -> bool as the only read boundary for this optional key: after schema registration it reads load_config().verify.enforce_plan_material_pins'
scenarios: In a test that supplies a tmp_path as repo_root with a local rebar.toml, the reader would ignore the supplied root and read the process-CWD config instead, making tests non-hermetic.
The enforcement flag would vary based on CWD rather than the project being attested, violating the stated per-project enforcement intent.
findings.suggested_fix: Either call `load_config(repo_root).verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` (thread `repo_root` through), or remove the `repo_root` parameter and match `mcp_readonly()` exactly. The former is correct if per-project config isolation is needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the `failure_kind` three-value enum (`resolution`, `io`, `reducer`) with the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` helper contract (e.g. define it as a `Literal` type or a table in the same Implementation bullet), and add a specific AC line asserting each value is used for its named failure class.
evidence: Plan text: 'Every non-absence failure emits structured warning event `plan_material_pin_target_unreadable` with `failure_kind` exactly `resolution`, `io`, or `reducer`'
The `TargetPinDetail` contract does not carry a `failure_kind` field, and no schema block or enum definition anchors the three values near the helper's contract or the structured-log spec.
An implementer writing the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` function must scan the prose to find the three-value vocabulary — it is not co-located with the function's contract definition.
findings.finding: `failure_kind` three-value vocabulary for `plan_material_pin_target_unreadable` warning event is named only once in prose and never co-located with the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` helper's contract or a schema block.
findings.location: Implementation section, bullet 3: `derive_plan_material_pin_health` helper definition
scenarios: Implementer classifies an I/O exception as 'resolution' or omits the field entirely — the test for AC 'non-missing read failures emit `plan_material_pin_target_unreadable`' would still pass if failure_kind is wrong.
A future reader extending error cases adds a fourth value without noticing the plan's fixed vocabulary.
findings.suggested_fix: In the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` bullet, add: '`failure_kind` values: `resolution` — target reference unresolvable or canonical-ID mismatch; `io` — filesystem/store read error; `reducer` — compiled-state reduction failure. These correspond 1-to-1 with `PlanRelationSnapshotError.reason` classes `missing-target`/`ambiguous-reference` → `resolution`, `store-read-failure` → `io`, `reducer-error` → `reducer`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the cross-section contradiction in AC#24: either reword it to 'DRIFT_REFRESH is wired to its call site in this story; CLOSE is defined with correct semantics and deferred to child 2bbd' (matching the Implementation), or explicitly scope the 'owning call site' assertion to DRIFT_REFRESH only for this story.
evidence: Implementation: 'make their call chain explicitly select `profile=PlanValidityProfile.DEFAULT`; child 2bbd later wires `CLOSE` into `transition_close`'
AC#24: '`CLOSE` and `DRIFT_REFRESH` each disable only code freshness in this story and remain separately selected by their owning call sites.'
CLOSE has no wired call site in this story per the Implementation, so 'separately selected by their owning call sites' cannot be verified for CLOSE within this story's test suite.
findings.finding: AC#24 asserts that both `CLOSE` and `DRIFT_REFRESH` are 'separately selected by their owning call sites' in this story, but the Implementation explicitly defers `CLOSE`'s only call site (`transition_close`) to child 2bbd, leaving CLOSE unwired in this story. A test verifying AC#24 as written cannot pass for CLOSE's call site within this story's scope.
findings.location: Implementation § PlanValidityProfile / AC: '`CLOSE` and `DRIFT_REFRESH` each disable only code freshness in this story and remain separately selected by their owning call sites.'
scenarios: An implementer writing the AC#24 test checks that CLOSE is selected somewhere — finds no call site — and either writes a vacuously passing test or concludes the AC is unverifiable.
A reviewer treating AC#24 as a hard gate blocks merge because CLOSE is unwired.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC#24 with: '`DRIFT_REFRESH` is wired to its call site and disables only code freshness; `CLOSE` is defined with the same code-freshness-only rule and its call site is deferred to child 2bbd — the two constants remain distinct so later policy changes cannot silently conflate them.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py:242` as an external caller of `resign_plan_review` that must be audited for return-shape compatibility after the resign.py changes.
evidence: src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py:242: return rebar.llm.resign_plan_review(ticket_id)
The plan enumerates return-shape consumers of compute_validity but does not enumerate consumers of resign_plan_review's return dict
_mcp_llm.py is outside the plan_review/ directory
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py:242` calls `rebar.llm.resign_plan_review(ticket_id)` — an external caller of `resign_plan_review` outside the `plan_review/` directory. The plan's edit to `resign.py` changes `resign_plan_review`'s behavior (adds sidecar pin reads, may change the return dict shape under enforcement), but `_mcp_llm.py` is not enumerated as a caller to audit for shape compatibility.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Edit resign.py' bullet; AC 'Re-sign preserves the existing own-material staleness guard and never passes a sidecar payload to compute_validity'
scenarios: If resign_plan_review's return dict gains keys or changes semantics under enforcement, _mcp_llm.py silently passes whatever it receives back to the MCP client, potentially exposing unexpected health data or a changed error shape.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `reviewed_related_material` rides in the existing `plan_review_result_v2` schema additively (requiring no guard change) or requires a new `plan_review_result_v3` schema; add an AC or implementation note that confirms `latest_review_result` will surface the field to `derive_plan_material_pin_health` in both forward (new sidecar) and backward (old sidecar) cases.
evidence: Plan: 'Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload with the exact review-time PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material records already collected before the LLM by child acaf. Serialize reviewed_related_material as an ordered list...'

sidecar.py:149-153 confirms reader guards: payload.get('schema') in ('plan_review_result_v1', 'plan_review_result_v2') — a new field riding in an existing v2 payload would be returned as-is by dict.get, but the plan never confirms this is the intended design (additive v2 field vs new v3 schema).

Plan: 'On read, derive_plan_material_pin_health validates list type, exact item keys, role, canonical ID, fingerprint syntax, order, and duplicate role-plus-ID' — this depends on the field being present in what latest_review_result returns.
ABSENCE: The plan names no schema version bump and has no AC or test that confirms the sidecar schema version handling for the new field.
findings.finding: The plan adds a new `reviewed_related_material` field to the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar and makes the re-sign recovery path depend on it, but never specifies whether a schema version bump is required or explicitly verifies that the existing `latest_review_result` reader will surface the new field to `derive_plan_material_pin_health`.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload with the exact review-time PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material records' and 'Edit resign.py: ... reads reviewed_related_material'
scenarios: If a new sidecar schema version (e.g. plan_review_result_v3) is needed to carry reviewed_related_material but is not introduced, the schema guard in latest_review_result silently omits it — re-sign recovery always sees None and falls back to legacy-unpinned, defeating the enforcement goal.
If the field is intended to ride in v2 additively (the simpler path), this should be explicitly affirmed in an AC so implementers don't accidentally introduce a v3 that old readers reject.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the sidecar section: 'The field is additive within the existing plan_review_result_v2 schema; no schema version bump is required. latest_review_result returns the full payload dict, so resign.py reads reviewed_related_material via payload.get("reviewed_related_material") — absent for v1/v2 sidecars written before this story, which yields legacy-unpinned.' This makes the capability assertion explicit and verifiable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `reviewed_related_material: []` from a ticket with genuinely zero relationships should produce `current` (all zero pins are trivially current) or `legacy-unpinned`, and add a dedicated AC line covering the zero-relationship case at resign.py.
evidence: Bullet 8: 'an absent field alone is legacy-unpinned' — this covers `None`/missing key.
Bullet 3 (`derive_plan_material_pin_health`): 'both `None` and an empty sequence produce `legacy-unpinned`' — this covers empty list.

Bullet 9 (resign.py): 'reads `reviewed_related_material`, and invokes `derive_plan_material_pin_health` on those records' — the normalization step (absent key → None → legacy-unpinned; present empty list → empty sequence → legacy-unpinned) is implied but not stated as an explicit step in the resign path.

A new sidecar for a ticket with literally zero children and zero prerequisites would legitimately emit `reviewed_related_material: []` — this should produce `current` (all zero pins are current), not `legacy-unpinned`. But the plan's rule 'empty sequence → legacy-unpinned' conflicts with this case.
findings.finding: The `resign.py` path for reading `reviewed_related_material` does not specify what behavior to produce when the sidecar field is a present-but-empty list `[]`, leaving an ambiguity between 'no relationships reviewed' and 'absent field = legacy-unpinned'.
findings.location: Implementation section, bullet 9 (resign.py) and bullet 8 (sidecar payload spec)
scenarios: A ticket with no children and no prerequisites gets a fresh review emitting `reviewed_related_material: []`; resign.py calls `derive_plan_material_pin_health([], ...)` which returns `legacy-unpinned`; enforcement-enabled resign incorrectly treats it as unenforceable rather than trivially current.

An older sidecar missing the field should be `legacy-unpinned`; a new sidecar with genuinely zero relationships should be `current`. The single rule 'empty sequence → legacy-unpinned' conflates these two cases.
findings.suggested_fix: Distinguish 'absent field' (key not in sidecar → `None` passed to helper → `legacy-unpinned`) from 'present empty list' (key present with `[]` → empty sequence → `current`, since there are no pins to drift). Update bullet 3 of the Implementation to read: 'a `None` input (absent field) produces `legacy-unpinned`; an empty sequence with the field present produces `current` with an empty `targets` list.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether concurrent `resign_plan_review` calls on the same ticket are safe (e.g., state that the operation is idempotent because the signed output is deterministic given the same sidecar content, or that the ticket store enforces single-writer semantics), so the TOCTOU window is explicitly addressed.
evidence: 'resign_plan_review ... reads reviewed_related_material, and invokes derive_plan_material_pin_health on those records before signing'
No locking, CAS, or idempotency guarantee is mentioned for the read-validate-sign-write sequence
T9 checklist: 'Shared/mutable state mutated atomically (lock/CAS/transaction, no check-then-act TOCTOU); operation idempotent under retry/at-least-once'
findings.finding: The resign flow introduces a read-validate-write sequence on the sidecar without specifying idempotency or concurrency safety. If two concurrent re-sign invocations run simultaneously on the same ticket, both could read the same sidecar, both could pass the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` validation, and both could write a new signed attestation, potentially with inconsistent results if the underlying targets change between reads.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Edit `resign.py`' paragraph; AC: 'Failed-sign recovery succeeds without an existing certified attestation when own material and review-time related material remain current'
scenarios: Two operators simultaneously invoke resign on the same stuck ticket; one reads stale material, the other reads current; the race determines which signed attestation lands
Automated retry of a failed resign call executes concurrently with the original, producing two writes

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add rationale for computing `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` before the reopen/registry/material early returns, or move health derivation to occur only after those O(1) guards pass (preserving cheapest-first ordering) while still including health in all return shapes.
evidence: Plan: 'parse pins and build DerivedPlanReviewHealth before all reopen, registry, code, and own-material early returns; every such plan-review return includes health'
Existing `compute_validity` applies reopen check (O(1) field read) as the first guard after the nil-attestation check, before any I/O
`derive_plan_material_pin_health` must resolve current target fingerprints (store reads) to populate `current_fingerprint` on each `TargetPinDetail`
Plan states 'A missing/deleted target or any target fingerprint read, resolution, I/O, or reducer failure produces stale-pin-missing' — confirming I/O is involved
findings.finding: The plan mandates building `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` (I/O-bound: resolves current target fingerprints for all related targets) before the O(1) reopen check inside `compute_validity`, inverting the existing cheapest-first early-exit ordering. On a reopened ticket with several pinned relations, the claim gate now performs unnecessary I/O before the reopen guard would have terminated cheaply. No rationale is given for this ordering choice.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 6: 'After signature certification and plan-review-kind validation, parse pins and build DerivedPlanReviewHealth before all reopen, registry, code, and own-material early returns'
scenarios: A ticket is reopened after a previously pinned plan review; the claim gate path now resolves N target fingerprints before the O(1) reopen check terminates it
At high claim-gate throughput, the added per-call I/O for health derivation on reopened or registry-stale attestations increases latency unnecessarily
findings.suggested_fix: Compute pin health after the reopen, registry-version, and signature checks pass (i.e., just before the code-drift and own-material checks), and attach it to every subsequent return. The reopen and regver checks are O(1) and should remain first guards. Paths that terminate on reopen/regver can return health as `legacy-unpinned` or a sentinel (e.g., `{pin_status: 'unchecked', enforced: false, targets: []}`) since pin validity is moot when the attestation is already invalid for a cheaper reason.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit post-rollback schema-state assertion: state that pre-feature code consuming a `plan_review_result_v2` sidecar carrying `reviewed_related_material` safely ignores the new field (mirroring child acaf's 'pre-feature reader ignores additive pin lines' AC), and add a test fixture proving this read-forward compatibility.
evidence: Plan says: 'Rollback removes the additive health key and its consumers; no stored-data migration is required.' — this addresses migration rollback but not the read-safety of already-written sidecar records by old code.
ABSENCE: no plan statement that old sidecar.py readers (pre-rollback) safely ignore `reviewed_related_material` in a `plan_review_result_v2` payload.

The signed-manifest case has an explicit AC in child acaf: 'A pre-feature reader authenticates the complete stored manifest, then ignores additive pin lines.' No equivalent assertion exists for the sidecar payload post-rollback.
findings.finding: The plan does not explicitly assert the post-rollback read behavior of REVIEW_RESULT sidecar events written by the new code (carrying `reviewed_related_material`) when consumed by rolled-back (pre-feature) code. T11 requires the plan to state the schema STATE after rollback, not merely that 'rollback exits 0' or 'no migration required.' A rolled-back reader encountering a new-format `plan_review_result_v2` payload with the extra field could malfunction if the sidecar reader is not permissive enough — this is safe in the current codebase (permissive dict access), but the plan itself provides no assertion.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload…' and rollback note 'Rollback removes the additive health key and its consumers; no stored-data migration is required.'
scenarios: Rollback is executed after some reviews have run new code and written `reviewed_related_material`-bearing sidecars. The `resign_plan_review` or `drift_refresh_candidate` flows (pre-rollback code) consume those sidecars. If the old reader has any exact-shape validation or key enumeration, it could misparse or reject a `v2` sidecar carrying the new field.
A partially deployed environment where some nodes run new code and some run old code simultaneously read each other's written sidecars.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC analogous to acaf's legacy-reader AC: 'A pre-feature sidecar reader (pre-`reviewed_related_material` code) consuming a new-format `plan_review_result_v2` payload treats the unknown field as absent and behaves identically to legacy-unpinned.' This should be covered by a test in `tests/unit/test_plan_review_sidecar.py` that feeds a `v2` payload with the new field to the pre-feature reader path.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
